metadata.basis: Frameworks listed in the shared ChatGPT conversation, filtered to permissive open-source licenses only. GitHub repository metadata and official documentation were checked on 2026-07-05.
metadata.score_scale: 0.0 to 5.0, where higher is better. implementation_ease is also higher-is-better: a high score means a smaller implementation burden.

criteria.implementation_ease: Setup effort, cognitive load, amount of glue code, and operational complexity for a practical prototype.
criteria.maturity: Release history, ecosystem traction, project age, and production posture.
criteria.provider_portability: Ability to use multiple model providers or local/open-weight models without a hard platform lock-in.
criteria.sandbox_isolation: Strength and clarity of execution isolation for agent-written commands and untrusted code.
criteria.persistence_memory: Native support for sessions, memory, durable state, context offloading, or resumability.
criteria.multi_agent: Support for subagents, parallel agents, supervisors, or orchestration across workstreams.
criteria.human_control: Human-in-the-loop approvals, review loops, steering, and interruptibility.
criteria.ci_pr: CI, GitHub, issue, branch, and pull-request automation fit.
criteria.observability: Logs, traces, telemetry, replayability, and debugging support.
criteria.security_governance: Permissions, policies, approval boundaries, or governance controls beyond basic sandboxing.
criteria.extensibility: Plugins, MCP, hooks, custom tools, skills, or framework-level extension points.
criteria.deployment_flexibility: Viability across local, self-hosted, cloud, CI, container, or remote execution targets.
criteria.coding_fit: Fit for software engineering tasks rather than generic chat only.
criteria.research_reproducibility: Usefulness for benchmarks, ablations, deterministic experiments, and explainable scaffolds.

excluded.name: Claude Agent SDK
excluded.reason: Not included in the permissive open-source shortlist from this analysis. The shared table marked it as an official Anthropic license and Claude-centric, so it does not meet the requested permissive OSS filter.

excluded.name: Codex app
excluded.reason: Not included because the shared table marked it as a closed/commercial desktop application, not an open-source framework.

alternatives.name: Sandcastle
alternatives.summary: TypeScript library for orchestrating coding agents in isolated sandboxes with branch/worktree strategies.
alternatives.implementation_notes: Best when the target is a programmable TypeScript runner around existing coding agents. Docker, Podman, Vercel, and no-sandbox modes make the isolation model explicit.
alternatives.risk_notes: Young beta project. Examples are strongly tied to Claude Code, and security depends on the chosen sandbox provider, mounts, and network policy.

alternatives.name: Flue
alternatives.summary: Programmable TypeScript agent harness with sessions, tools, skills, sandboxing, deployment targets, and observability adapters.
alternatives.implementation_notes: Strong candidate for building a product-specific orchestrator in TypeScript. It has a clearer framework shape than a CLI wrapper.
alternatives.risk_notes: Beta API surface and Node.js >=22.19.0 requirement. Durable persistence requires choosing and operating a backing store.

alternatives.name: Anchor
alternatives.summary: Narrow review-harness/plugin style project centered on Claude Code workflows.
alternatives.implementation_notes: Small surface area can be useful for a deterministic review loop, not for a full orchestrator.
alternatives.risk_notes: Very low traction, narrow vendor focus, and no strong sandbox or orchestration layer of its own.

alternatives.name: Omnigent
alternatives.summary: Meta-harness for orchestrating multiple coding-agent backends with policies, sandboxing, and collaboration.
alternatives.implementation_notes: Compelling for a control-plane strategy where multiple agent harnesses must be swapped or combined.
alternatives.risk_notes: Alpha status and broad scope make it riskier than narrower libraries. Operational complexity can be high.

alternatives.name: OmniAgent
alternatives.summary: Local multi-model AI coding agent CLI with ReAct, Plan-Execute, MCP, memory, and TUI concepts.
alternatives.implementation_notes: Potentially hackable Python CLI, but not a strong base for governed orchestration yet.
alternatives.risk_notes: No stars at retrieval time, early release, and weak evidence for sandboxing, CI, observability, or enterprise controls.

alternatives.name: Omni Agent
alternatives.summary: Verification-native local coding-agent runtime with eval gates, memory, subagents, and model profiles.
alternatives.implementation_notes: Interesting claims for verification-first design, but evidence and traction are too thin for a primary adoption bet.
alternatives.risk_notes: Minimal traction and no release at retrieval time. Treat as a reference idea, not a dependable platform.

alternatives.name: Deep Agents
alternatives.summary: Batteries-included agent harness with tools, filesystem, permissions, code execution, memory, subagents, and human-in-the-loop controls.
alternatives.implementation_notes: One of the strongest foundations for a custom Python orchestrator, especially when LangGraph/LangSmith integration matters.
alternatives.risk_notes: Not a turnkey coding product by itself. Production use requires designing tools, permissions, sandbox backends, and evaluation loops.

alternatives.name: Codex CLI
alternatives.summary: OpenAI terminal coding agent with OS-level sandboxing, approvals, subagents, skills, hooks, and GitHub Action integration.
alternatives.implementation_notes: Excellent secure local/CI runner if OpenAI dependence is acceptable. Less suitable as a fully provider-neutral framework.
alternatives.risk_notes: Provider center of gravity is OpenAI. Some extension and multi-agent surfaces may still evolve quickly.

alternatives.name: OpenCode
alternatives.summary: Open-source coding agent for terminal, desktop, and IDE workflows with broad provider support.
alternatives.implementation_notes: Very strong for local developer experience and model portability. Less policy-first than sandbox-oriented frameworks.
alternatives.risk_notes: Native sandbox/governance is lighter than Codex, Sandcastle, Open SWE, or OpenHands SDK; isolation often depends on the surrounding environment.

alternatives.name: Cline / Cline SDK
alternatives.summary: Autonomous coding agent delivered as SDK, IDE extension, CLI, and automation surface.
alternatives.implementation_notes: Strong practical DX and human approval model. Good when the product needs editor/CLI/SDK surfaces together.
alternatives.risk_notes: Sandboxing is more approval/checkpoint oriented than hard isolation. Enterprise controls must be configured deliberately.

alternatives.name: OpenHands Agent Canvas
alternatives.summary: Self-hosted developer control center for running coding agents and automations locally or in the cloud.
alternatives.implementation_notes: Best evaluated as a control plane/UI layer, not as the core agent algorithm. It can run OpenHands, Codex, Claude Code, Gemini, or ACP-compatible backends.
alternatives.risk_notes: Young project with low star count. The actual security boundary is determined by backend selection and deployment topology.

alternatives.name: OpenHands Software Agent SDK
alternatives.summary: Composable Python and REST SDK for building software-engineering agents with local or ephemeral workspaces.
alternatives.implementation_notes: A strong Python base for building custom software agents where coding-specific tools, workspaces, and benchmark orientation matter.
alternatives.risk_notes: Less minimal than Aider or mini-SWE-agent. Teams must still define policies, toolsets, and deployment boundaries.

alternatives.name: Open SWE
alternatives.summary: Open-source asynchronous coding-agent framework built on Deep Agents and LangGraph, with cloud sandbox providers and automatic PR creation.
alternatives.implementation_notes: Strong fit for internal async coding-agent platforms. It is more platform starter than simple CLI.
alternatives.risk_notes: Requires operating sandbox providers and integration surfaces. Hosted examples have historically leaned Anthropic, so model portability should be verified for the target stack.

alternatives.name: Aider
alternatives.summary: Terminal pair-programming agent focused on Git-based code edits, repo maps, and broad model support.
alternatives.implementation_notes: Best quick path for local pair programming and lightweight automation. Very easy to trial.
alternatives.risk_notes: Not designed as a full multi-agent governance platform. Sandboxing and policy controls are mostly external.

alternatives.name: goose
alternatives.summary: General-purpose local AI agent with desktop, CLI, API, MCP extensions, and broad provider support.
alternatives.implementation_notes: Good local automation substrate when tasks extend beyond code and MCP extension breadth matters.
alternatives.risk_notes: Native observability and coding-specific orchestration are lighter than specialized coding frameworks.

alternatives.name: SWE-agent
alternatives.summary: Research-grade and practical harness for solving real GitHub issues with a model of choice.
alternatives.implementation_notes: Strong when benchmarkability, issue-resolution tasks, and trajectory logs matter more than polished product UX.
alternatives.risk_notes: Research-first developer experience. Less suitable as a low-friction daily coding assistant or enterprise control plane.

alternatives.name: mini-SWE-agent
alternatives.summary: Minimal Python SWE agent for solving GitHub issues or command-line tasks, optimized for simplicity and benchmark reproducibility.
alternatives.implementation_notes: Best scaffold for learning, ablations, and small research experiments. Very easy to reason about.
alternatives.risk_notes: Minimal platform features by design: weak native governance, persistence, multi-agent, and observability compared with larger frameworks.
